name: autonomous-loop-iteration-limit
description: Claudecode /goal with an unsatisfiable condition; the recipe lets the loop run a few iterations then sends /goal clear as a stand-in for the max_iterations cap (which is not deterministically forceable today). The state arc on cap-hit and on /goal clear is identical: end_turn followed by ready with no further Stop-hook re-prompts. Tests the loop's graceful-termination shape from the spec.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: partial
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: partial
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

The scenario's **Primitive exercised** is "iteration cap enforcement — the agent CLI has a configurable max-step limit and emits a clean turn-end signal when it hits the cap" (maps to `autonomous_mode` in `capabilities.json`). The **User-observable signal** is narrow and is the only thing the spec asserts: state reaches `ready` even though the agent did not fully succeed, and does NOT stick in `working`.

**agent_supports: partial.** Aider DOES have a bounded autonomous iteration loop — the *reflection loop* in `coders/base_coder.py:run_one()`. After `send_message`, if the model's output triggers a follow-up (lint failure, test failure, or an edit/format error), aider auto-generates a `reflected_message` and re-prompts the model WITHOUT user intervention. The loop is hard-capped: `max_reflections = 3` (base_coder.py:101). On hitting the cap it stops with `tool_warning("Only 3 reflections allowed, stopping.")` (base_coder.py:939-941) and returns from the turn. This is a genuine self-prompting loop with a max-step limit and a clean turn-end on cap — the primitive the scenario tests.

It is `partial` rather than `yes` for two reasons: (1) the cap is hardcoded at 3 and is NOT user-configurable via any CLI flag, env var, or settings key (`aider --help` exposes no `--max-reflections` / `--max-iterations`; the source constant is the only knob). (2) It is a *reflection / error-recovery* loop, not an open-ended goal-pursuit mode like claudecode's `/goal` — aider only iterates when the model's own output fails validation; you cannot direct it to keep working toward an arbitrary condition. Note: `capabilities.json` for aider has no `autonomous_mode` key at all (the Primitive's canonical key is absent), which is itself consistent with `partial` — aider has only this incidental reflection loop, not a first-class autonomous mode. This matches the existing matrix verdict of `partial`.

## daemon_capability: full

The aider adapter is `FilesUnderCWD` over the markdown transcript `.aider.chat.history.md`, parsed by a `RawParser` (core/adapters/inbound/agents/aider/parser.go). Tracing the capped reflection loop through the parser:

- The whole loop is ONE turn: `io.user_input()` writes the `#### ` history header exactly once per top-level prompt; `run_one`'s `while reflected_message:` loop calls `send_message` directly and never re-emits a `#### ` header. The parser opens the turn on the single `#### ` line (parser.go:94-108, `turnOpen=true`).
- Each reflection iteration's model call lands as a `> Tokens: …` line, which the parser maps to `assistant_message` and explicitly KEEPS the turn open (`closeModelCall`, parser.go:115-117,153-188; the comment at L150-152/L174 documents that multiple `> Tokens:` per turn is the normal `--yes-always` case). So the session stays continuously `working` across all reflection rounds — no flicker.
- `> Applied edit to …` and `> Running …` markers synthesize `tool_result` events (parser.go:119-124,231-242) which also do not move state off `working`.
- The turn ends the same way EVERY normal aider turn ends: the transcript goes quiet, `IdleFlush` fires after 1500ms (parser.go:216-229) and synthesizes a payload-free `turn_done`, flipping `LastEventType` so the state classifier transitions `working → ready`.

The single spec-required, user-observable signal — "state reaches `ready`, does not stick in `working`" — is therefore observed correctly. The daemon emits ≥2 turns of activity (`min_turns: 2` via the per-call `> Tokens:` assistant_messages) and a final `ready` (matching the `verify` block). Because the spec asserts only the termination SHAPE (and our scenarios are user-observable-only — no internal reasons or event kinds), the daemon meets every required observable today: **daemon_capability: full**.

The one thing the daemon CANNOT do is DISCRIMINATE "loop hit its cap" from "turn completed normally": both terminate via the identical idle-flush `turn_done`, and the cap-specific `> Only 3 reflections allowed, stopping.` blockquote is silently ignored by the parser (its `errorRE`, parser.go:75, matches only `Error`/`Exception`, so the line falls through to the catch-all blockquote branch at parser.go:126-132 and returns nil). Per #476 / the cell-lifecycle authoring rule, this is NOT a `bug` and NOT `incapable` — the spec does not require a cap-specific signal, so it is a **caveat** (feature-invisible-but-spec-compliant), and `daemon_capability` stays `full`. Reserving `bug` for a spec-required observable the daemon mis-handles is exactly the discipline #476 introduced; downgrading here would re-create the old `partial`-dumping-ground.

## driver_capability: ready

The recipe's step grammar is `send → wait_turn → wait_turn → sleep`, all of which `scripts/drive-aider-interactive.sh` implements (send / wait_turn / sleep are in the driver's supported set; this cell needs none of the gapped primitives — restart, reset_session, resume, start_session, exit_clean, sigkill, keys, session, fork). The reflection loop is driven to its cap deterministically by `--auto-test --test-cmd false` (every edit trips a non-zero test exit → forced reflected_message until max_reflections=3), with no arrow-key picker or forced-kill step required. **driver_capability: ready** — matching the coverage row.

## #476 re-derivation note

Old schema: `irrlicht_observes: partial`. Split honestly: the blocker was never a missing driver step (driver grammar is complete → `driver_capability: ready`), and the daemon DOES represent the only spec-required observable (the graceful `working → ready`) → `daemon_capability: full`. The cap-vs-normal indistinguishability — the part that drove the old `partial` — is a non-spec-required gap, so it lives in caveats, not in a downgraded axis. This mirrors the canonical anti-pattern guidance: caveats over downgrades.

## What would let irrlicht surface the cap specifically (not required by this scenario)

1. The aider adapter extracts the `Only N reflections allowed, stopping.` blockquote into a first-class daemon signal (e.g. a turn-end reason), letting irrlicht assert "iteration cap reached" specifically rather than only "loop ended."
2. Aider exposes the cap as a CLI knob (`--max-reflections`) so a recording could force the cap deterministically at a low value instead of relying on an auto-test that keeps failing.
caveats: feature-invisible-but-spec-compliant: the cap-hit warning `> Only 3 reflections allowed, stopping.` is written to the transcript but the parser ignores it (its errorRE matches only Error/Exception, so the line returns nil at parser.go:126-132). The daemon observes the graceful working→ready termination but cannot distinguish a cap-hit from a normal turn completion — both terminate via the same idle-flush turn_done. The scenario asserts only the termination shape, so this does not block daemon_capability:full.

Aider's loop is a reflection / error-recovery loop (re-prompt on lint/test/edit failure), not an open-ended goal-pursuit autonomous mode. max_reflections=3 is hardcoded in base_coder.py with no CLI/env/settings override, so a recording cannot pre-lower the cap; the recipe must drive the loop to its cap via repeated auto-test failures (--auto-test --test-cmd false). This is the reason agent_supports is partial, not yes.

KNOWN TIMING RISK: end-of-turn is ALWAYS synthesized by the 1500ms IdleFlush (clamped by the SessionDetector ~5s working-refresh poll). If any single inter-reflection model call leaves the transcript silent >~1.5s, IdleFlush can synthesize a premature turn_done (working→ready) and the next `> Tokens:` line reopens it (ready→working) — a flicker. Root cause is timing, not topology; a fast local model avoids it.

Distinct from token-quota-exhausted (a cost/budget limit) and turn-aborted-by-error (an unplanned provider error). This scenario tests the planned, count-based reflection cap whose terminal signal is a clean turn-end, not an exception.

sources.note: aider 0.86.2. run_one() reflection loop (L922-943): while reflected_message, re-prompt; max_reflections=3 (L101); on cap, tool_warning('Only 3 reflections allowed, stopping.') then return. Confirms the bounded self-prompting iteration loop and its clean turn-end on cap (grounds agent_supports:partial).

tool_warning -> _tool_message -> append_chat_history(blockquote=True): the cap-hit warning lands in .aider.chat.history.md as a `> …` blockquote line. Confirms the cap signal IS written to the watched Source (so the gap is parser-side, not transport-side).

FilesUnderCWD markdown RawParser. `#### ` opens one turn (L94-108); `> Tokens:` -> assistant_message keeps turn open (L115-117,153-188); errorRE (L75) matches only Error/Exception so the `stopping` line returns nil at the catch-all blockquote branch (L126-132); IdleFlush (L216-229) synthesizes the only end-of-turn turn_done after 1500ms -> working→ready. Grounds daemon_capability:full for the spec-required termination shape AND the caveat that cap-hit is not a distinct signal.

Confirms aider Source variant is FilesUnderCWD over `.aider.chat.history.md` with a RawLineParser (does not implement PendingContributor/SubagentCounter) — the transport that grounds the daemon_capability claim.

Step 4 Primitive-exercised confirmation: the scenario's primitive maps to `autonomous_mode`, but aider's capabilities.json has NO autonomous_mode key (headless_mode:true is the recipe's `requires`). Absence of a first-class autonomous mode is consistent with agent_supports:partial (only an incidental reflection loop).

Step 2.5 surface check: --help exposes --auto-commits/--auto-lint/--auto-test/--auto-accept-architect but NO --max-reflections / --max-iterations / autonomous-loop flag. The cap is the hardcoded base_coder.py constant only — supports the 'partial' (not user-configurable) verdict.
Sibling worked example for the same scenario: the cap-hit path is indistinguishable from the normal end-of-loop path. Same reasoning anchors the aider caveat.

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against its configured provider/model (auth is set up out-of-band; the rig's precheck delegates auth to the user). The live REPL needs a model that actually answers so the reflection loop really iterates inside one turn; headless aider with no reachable provider hangs ~5min on the model-auth/OAuth prompt — which is why a recording for this cell is deferred until auth is available in the environment.

tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so the process outlives the whole capped reflection turn (the terminal working→ready settle is idle-flush-bound and must be observed before teardown)
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

agent_supports:partial scope_note (mirror of the coverage matrix): aider's ONLY autonomous self-prompting loop is the reflection loop in coders/base_coder.py run_one() — it auto-re-prompts the model on auto-lint/auto-test/edit failure and is hard-capped at max_reflections=3 (base_coder.py:101). It is partial (not yes) because the cap is a hardcoded constant with NO --max-reflections/--max-iterations CLI/env/settings override (so the recipe must DRIVE the loop to its cap via repeated lint/test failures, it cannot pre-lower the cap), AND it is a reflection/error-recovery loop rather than an open-ended goal-pursuit mode like claudecode's /goal — aider iterates only when its own output fails validation.

irrlicht_observes:partial scope_note (mirror of the coverage matrix): irrlicht observes the graceful-termination SHAPE (working→ready, no stuck working) but CANNOT distinguish a cap-hit from a normal turn-end — both terminate via the identical 1500ms IdleFlush turn_done. The cap warning `> Only 3 reflections allowed, stopping.` IS written to the transcript but the parser's errorRE matches only Error/Exception (not 'stopping'), so it is ignored; no cap-specific daemon signal is emitted. The recipe therefore asserts only the user-observable termination shape, not 'cap reached' specifically.

KNOWN TIMING RISK (observes:partial): the reflection loop is genuinely WITHIN one turn (one `#### ` header, run_one's `while reflected_message:` loop, max_reflections=3), so the topology is one contiguous working span. The only thing that can break the no-flicker target is the 1500ms IdleFlush: if any single inter-reflection model call leaves the transcript silent for >~1.5s, IdleFlush synthesizes a premature turn_done (working→ready) and the next `> Tokens:` line reopens it (ready→working). A fast local model avoids this; a slow remote model can trip it. The root cause is timing, not topology — do NOT widen the spec to accept the flicker.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches aider in a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in .aider.chat.history.md before sending the prompt

HOW the reflection is elicited deterministically (mirror of the aider/self-correction-iteration sibling that validated 6/6): settings.aider_extra_args launches aider with `--auto-test --test-cmd false`. `false` always exits non-zero, so EVERY edit aider makes inside the turn trips a test failure and forces a reflected_message — independent of what gpt-5-nano actually writes. The loop re-prompts until it hits max_reflections=3, then stops with `> Only 3 reflections allowed, stopping.` and returns; the turn always settles to ready, so we never depend on the model producing passing code. The driver appends these flags to its fixed arg set (--no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore [+ --model]); the step grammar (send/wait_turn/sleep) is fully supported (no driver_gap). The send asks for a small file edit aider can reliably perform, which is what arms --auto-test; it deliberately does NOT request a shell command — aider's reflection loop fires on --auto-lint/--auto-test failure, not on a model-invoked shell tool's exit code.

aider stores its transcript as .aider.chat.history.md in the cwd (FilesUnderCWD, RawLineParser), so the daemon keys the WHOLE session by proc-<PID> — there is no separate transcript-UUID rollout session and no handoff/reconcile; the turn arc, pid_discovered and teardown all land on that one proc-<PID> row. The transcript is LAZY (materializes on first input), but the proc-<PID> pre-session is observable from launch (that is where the spec's session_birth 'ready within 1s' is grounded)

WHY the capped loop is ONE turn: io.user_input() writes the `#### ` history header exactly once per top-level prompt, before Coder.run_one; run_one's reflection loop sets reflected_message and calls send_message directly, never re-emitting a `#### ` header (base_coder.py:860/879/924-944). The parser treats every `> Tokens:` line as assistant_message (NOT turn_done) so the turn stays open across the multiple capped model calls (parser.go:18-23,146-188), and `> Running <cmd>`→tool_result markers never change state away from working — so the whole capped reflection chain is one contiguous ready→working→ready span

No auto-mode / permission step is needed and there is no gate to enable: aider runs under --yes-always and has NO permission hook (capabilities.json permission_hooks:false), so any shell-command confirmation auto-answers and the session never enters waiting — unlike the claudecode sibling for this cell, which must enable auto mode to keep its Stop-hook loop alive without tripping a gate

WHY interactive, not headless `aider --message`: aider emits no end-of-turn marker, so working→ready is synthesized by IdleFlush after a ~1.5s quiet window, clamped to the SessionDetector's ~5s working-refresh poll; a headless --message run can exit promptly after the reply, so the daemon misses the terminal working→ready settle before process_exited and truncates the arc. Keeping aider alive in the REPL across one send + wait_turn lets the daemon observe the full ready→working→ready settle cleanly, and the single tmux kill-session teardown then yields one clean process_exited

wait_turn blocks until a new `> Tokens:` line appears in the transcript (aider's per-round close marker — one per model call inside the reflection loop). The recipe waits TWICE (same as the self-correction sibling): the FIRST wait_turn returns on the initial edit's `> Tokens:`, the SECOND returns on the first reflection round's `> Tokens:` (the auto-test failed and aider re-called the model). This guarantees the reflection loop actually entered before teardown — a single wait_turn returns too early (the first reflection call is still in flight). The ≥20s trailing sleep then absorbs the remaining capped reflection rounds (max_reflections=3) plus one full ~1.5s IdleFlush cycle so the terminal working→ready settle is observed before the single tmux kill-session teardown

script.text: Create a new file loop.py containing a single Python function add(a, b) that returns a + b. Make the edit now; do not ask me anything.

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new + presession_created for the proc-<PID> session born ready within ~1s of REPL launch
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the same proc-<PID> session_id

events.jsonl shows exactly one state_transition→working on the proc-<PID> session_id when the prompt is sent, sustained across the capped reflection iterations (auto-lint/auto-test keep failing → up to max_reflections=3 re-prompts, all inside one turn)

events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to ready between reflection iterations — the session stays continuously working across the loop (the parser holds working across `> Tokens:`/`> Running` runs within one `#### ` turn)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point (aider has no permission hook; runs under --yes-always)

events.jsonl shows exactly one working→ready transition at the single turn end (synthesized by IdleFlush ~1.5s after the last `> Tokens:` when the loop hits its cap and stops self-prompting) — the user-observable signal: state reaches ready even though the goal was not fully met, and does NOT stick in working
the session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the proc-<PID> session at REPL teardown

OBSERVES:PARTIAL — the cap-hit is NOT distinguishable from a normal turn-end: the `> Only 3 reflections allowed, stopping.` warning is written to the transcript but the parser ignores it (errorRE matches only Error/Exception), so do NOT assert a 'cap reached' signal — assert only the graceful working→ready termination shape

KNOWN TIMING RISK (observes:partial): if any single inter-reflection model call is silent >~1.5s, IdleFlush can fire a premature working→ready then the next `> Tokens:` reopens working→working flicker — flag it as a known timing limitation, do NOT widen the spec to accept it
Do not assert the auto-lint/auto-test output text — it is model/tool/OS-dependent; assert only the state topology and the single graceful turn end

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

This is the failure-mode pair of `autonomous-loop`. The sibling assessment (corrected 2026-05-18) establishes that claudecode DOES support `/goal` autonomous self-prompting via a Stop-hook re-prompt mechanism. So the precondition for iteration-limit (an autonomous loop exists at all) is satisfied for claudecode.

**The spec's three Expected: bullets:**

1. `working → ready` when the cap hits.
2. State reaches `ready` even though the agent didn't fully succeed.
3. State does not stick in `working` indefinitely.

All three are observable in claudecode for the broader 'goal ended without met:true' family of termination events:

- `/goal clear` (user-driven termination before goal met) produces a clean `working → ready` final transition once the in-flight iteration completes — that's the recipe used for THIS recording.
- A natural goal-condition self-judgement (`goal_status` met:true) also produces `working → ready` at the same point.
- A hard iteration cap (claude binary's strings show references to `max_iterations` and `MaxIterations`) would produce the same final shape: end_turn → state goes ready, Stop-hook does NOT re-prompt because the cap is hit, state stays ready.

The spec's intent is to verify that the loop ends gracefully without sticking — that intent is observable regardless of which termination path the recipe exercises. irrlicht's parser treats end_turn → ready identically across all three paths because they share the same end_turn signal in the JSONL.

**Why `daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready` instead of `yes`:**

- Deterministically triggering a true `max_iterations` cap inside a recording is hard: claudecode doesn't expose the numeric cap via `--help` or settings, and a recording can't pre-set the agent's internal counter close to the limit. The recipe uses `/goal clear` after a budgeted sleep as a stand-in for the cap-hit path; the validator can't distinguish 'cap reached' from 'user cleared' from the daemon's events alone — both look like end_turn → ready with no further iterations.
- The `goal_status.sentinel` field that surfaces in the transcript attachment carries the termination reason (met / not met / cleared / capped) but the claudecode adapter does not extract it as a first-class daemon event today. This is a follow-up enhancement.

In short: the SHAPE of the cap-hit path is observable and the recording exercises an equivalent termination event (`/goal clear` after multiple iterations). The DISCRIMINATION between cap-hit and user-cleared is not.

## What would change this verdict to yes

1. Claudecode adapter extracts `goal_status.sentinel` (or equivalent) into a first-class daemon event with the termination reason. Then irrlicht could assert 'iteration cap reached' specifically, not just 'loop ended'.
2. The recipe gets a deterministic way to force the iteration cap (env var, --max-iterations flag, settings.json key). Then the recording could trigger the true cap-hit path.
caveats: Recording uses /goal clear after several iterations as the stand-in for max_iterations being hit. The state arc is identical (working → ready with no further iterations) but the proximate cause differs. A genuine cap-hit recording is future work, gated on either an adapter-side event extraction or a CLI knob to force the limit.

Distinct from token-quota-exhausted: the 5-hour subscription budget interrupts a single user-driven turn, which is a different failure mode (one-shot, not loop-based). This scenario tests the loop's iteration-cap path specifically.

sources.note: Strings include `max_iterations`, `MaxIterations`, and `goal_status` attachment with sentinel field — confirms a cap mechanism exists. The exact numeric cap and the conditions under which it fires are not surfaced in --help.
Sibling assessment establishes /goal exists and uses Stop-hook re-prompts. This scenario inherits the loop primitive and tests its termination shape.
Feature: Autonomous loop iteration limit reached L170-178 — the three Expected: bullets are observable for any 'loop ends without met:true' termination, which /goal clear demonstrates.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated
/goal requires a trusted workspace and hooks enabled
setup: Driver generates a fresh UUID + cwd

script.text: /goal You have replied with the integer that is both odd and even simultaneously. This goal is impossible. Keep guessing.

verify: events.jsonl contains at least 2 working->ready transitions on the same UUID
final state of the session is ready after /goal clear

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: partial
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: partial
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

This cell is the termination twin of the codex `autonomous-loop` cell (recorded 10/10). Codex (0.130.0) ships no built-in autonomous self-prompting loop — there is no `/goal`-style Stop-hook re-prompt, no full-auto, and no native `max_iterations` cap to hit. `autonomous_mode` is not among codex's declared capabilities (capabilities.json has no key; the closest, headless_mode, is single-shot `codex exec`). So codex cannot enforce its own iteration cap.

What codex CAN model — and what scenario-meanings' Essence for autonomous-loop-iteration-limit actually targets — is the GRACEFUL TERMINATION SHAPE: a loop runs a few iterations and then STOPS at its limit, and the session resolves to `ready` cleanly rather than getting stuck in `working`. For an adapter with no native autonomy, the loop body is supplied externally (driver send → wait_turn → sleep, repeated) exactly as in the autonomous-loop sibling. The 'iteration limit' is modeled by the driver simply NOT sending a further prompt after the last iteration: the loop terminates, and a long terminal idle sleep proves the session settles to ready and DWELLS there with no further self-prompted turns.

The irrlicht-relevant primitive is identical to the spec's: after the loop terminates, does the session land in `ready` and stay there, with no stuck-working and no spurious post-limit `working` flip? That is fully observable on a turn-delimited adapter.

agent_supports is **partial**: codex models the cap-hit termination only because the loop is externally driven; it has no native cap to enforce. This mirrors the autonomous-loop sibling's partial verdict.

## Observability

irrlicht_observes: **yes**. Each loop iteration is a normal codex turn on the live rollout session: ready → working at iteration start, working → ready at iteration end. The daemon sees the proc-PID pre-session born ready, the rollout session reconcile (new session_id) born ready, pid bound, then N clean per-iteration working/ready arcs on the SAME rollout session_id. The TERMINATION is the new assertion vs the sibling: after the final iteration's working→ready, a long terminal idle window (≥7s) during which the state DWELLS in ready with NO further state_transition to working — the loop has hit its limit and stopped self-prompting. No `waiting` state (codex has no permission gates: permission_hooks=false). The recording ends in the dwelling ready state (no REPL teardown asserted as the terminal — the dwell is the point), though a process_exited may follow when run-cell.sh tears the REPL down after the recording window.

## Why not n/a

Not n/a: the externally-driven loop plus terminal idle dwell is fully expressible with codex's interactive driver (send/wait_turn/sleep all supported) and the resulting recording exercises exactly the irrlicht primitive the scenario cares about (clean cross-iteration cycling, then a terminal ready that dwells with no further working transitions). It is NOT an orchestrator-only concept — a single codex session iterating then settling is a legitimate single-agent recording.
caveats: Codex has no native autonomous mode and no native max-iteration cap; the loop body is supplied externally by the driver and the 'limit' is modeled by the driver simply stopping (no further prompt) after the last iteration. The recording models the graceful-termination SHAPE, not codex enforcing its own cap. Documented in the recipe description so a re-record diff doesn't read as a regression.

The distinguishing assertion vs the autonomous-loop sibling is the terminal idle DWELL: a long final sleep (≥7s) with an invariant of no further state_transition to working, proving the session settles to ready and stays there after the limit is reached.
Mind the classifier debounce: ≥4s sleep between wait_turn and the next send so consecutive iterations register as distinct working→ready arcs rather than one coalesced turn.

sources.note: No autonomous_mode key; headless_mode is single-shot codex exec, permission_hooks=false (no waiting gates), slash_commands=true, session_resume=true.

Essence: autonomous-mode agent hits its max-iteration cap before completing; irrlicht resolves to ready cleanly rather than getting stuck in working. User-observable signal: state reaches ready, does not stick in working.
Sibling cell (10/10): externally-driven loop modeled as per-iteration working→ready arcs on one rollout session. This cell adds the terminal idle dwell asserting no post-limit working flip.
Driver supports send|slash, wait_turn, sleep, interrupt, keys, reset_session, fork, exit_clean, resume — every step type the externally-driven loop-then-idle recipe needs.

preconditions: codex CLI on PATH and authenticated
irrlichd is recording (--attach or isolated mode)

Codex has NO built-in autonomous self-prompting loop AND no native max-iteration cap; the loop is driven EXTERNALLY by the driver feeding successive prompts to the same live REPL session, and the 'iteration limit' is modeled by the driver simply NOT sending a further prompt after the last iteration
setup: Driver launches one interactive codex REPL; the rollout session reconciles to its own session_id once the first prompt lands. No trust dialog / settings file (codex has none).

script.text: We are running an iterative loop that stops at iteration 2 (its limit). This is iteration 1 of 2 — run `echo 1` and report that you are continuing to iteration 2. Do not do anything beyond echo 1.
Final loop iteration 2 of 2: run `echo 2`. This is the iteration limit — after this, stop. Confirm you have reached the limit and will not iterate further, then wait.

verify: events.jsonl contains the proc-PID pre-session born ready, then the rollout session reconcile (new session_id) born ready, then a pid_discovered for the rollout session
events.jsonl contains 2 working->ready transitions on the SAME rollout session_id — one clean cycle per externally-fed loop iteration before the limit

after the final iteration's working->ready, the session DWELLS in ready for the entire 8s trailing idle window with NO further state_transition to working — the loop stopped at its limit and did not stick in working or spuriously re-prompt
final state of the session is ready (the loop terminated cleanly at its limit)
no state_transition to waiting (codex has no permission gates)

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: unknown
daemon_capability: n/a
driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: unknown
daemon_capability: n/a
driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

`autonomous-loop-iteration-limit` is the autonomous self-prompting loop that STOPS at a configured iteration cap. It PRESUPPOSES the `autonomous-loop` primitive — an agent-native mode that self-prompts across internally-generated turn boundaries with no user message between iterations — and then asserts that the loop terminates gracefully when it reaches a max-iterations limit. With no autonomous loop, there is no iteration limit to observe.

Stock OpenCode has no such autonomous self-prompting / goal-loop mode (this is the same root finding as the sibling `autonomous-loop` cell, just confirmed):

- OpenCode's documented model is turn-based: a turn ends at `session.idle` (step-finish) and the REPL waits for the next user prompt. There is no `/goal`, no `/loop`, and no `--auto` flag — and therefore no built-in iteration counter or max-iterations cap to hit.
- A `strings $(which opencode)` scan for `autonomous`, `goal`, `self-prompt`, `full-auto`, `max_iterations`, `iteration limit`, and slash-command markers came up empty — the only `loop` hits are bundler / WASI / HTTP-508 ("Loop Detected") / Node event-loop noise from the bun-compiled binary, not a feature surface.
- The only autonomous self-prompting behaviors that exist ("Ralph Loop", "Ultrawork"/"Beast Mode") are THIRD-PARTY plugins (oh-my-opencode) that hook `session.idle` and re-call `session.prompt` — they are not part of stock opencode, and the upstream built-in `/loop` feature request (anomalyco/opencode#23578) is still OPEN.

Because the agent lacks the autonomous-loop primitive this cell is built on, there is no native iteration cap, no cap-hit termination shape to record, and `agent_supports` is `unknown` (a plugin-only path is not a stock-agent capability). The driver itself (send/sleep/wait_turn) is `ready` but has no self-loop to drive and no cap to reach. With no agent-native loop to observe, the observability question is moot, so `daemon_capability` is `n/a`.

## Driver / observability note

If a future opencode version (or a sanctioned plugin) shipped a real headless-drivable loop WITH a configurable iteration cap, the daemon's observability would still need re-checking: opencode's adapter is `ProcessOwnedStore` (SQLite, `core/adapters/inbound/agents/opencode/agent.go`), where the daemon polls a structured store rather than tailing JSONL, so whether the cap-hit settles cleanly to a terminal ready (vs. sticking in working or spuriously re-prompting) is empirically uncertain and must be measured from a live recording — not predicted from docs. That re-assessment is out of scope while the agent itself lacks the mode.
caveats: iteration-limit presupposes the autonomous-loop primitive opencode lacks; with no loop there is no max-iterations cap to hit and no graceful cap-termination shape to record.
Autonomous self-prompting in opencode exists only via third-party plugins (oh-my-opencode Ralph Loop / Ultrawork / Beast Mode), not stock opencode.

An upstream feature request for a built-in /loop command (anomalyco/opencode#23578) is still open — re-assess if it merges, and note that even then a configurable iteration CAP would need to exist for this specific cell.

sources.note: sibling autonomous-loop cell — same root finding: stock opencode has no autonomous self-prompting loop; this cell presupposes that primitive
features.headless_mode=false; no autonomous_mode key — stock opencode declares no autonomous-loop capability, so no iteration cap on one
Source is ProcessOwnedStore (SQLite); observability of a cap-hit terminal-ready settle would need a live recording, not doc inference
official CLI docs — turn-based REPL, no /goal, no /loop, no --auto, no max-iterations cap
open feature request to ADD a /loop command — confirms core opencode has no built-in autonomous loop, hence no iteration limit
autonomous self-prompting is a third-party oh-my-opencode plugin hooking session.idle to re-prompt — not stock opencode

recipe.notes: scope_note: opencode has no autonomous self-prompting loop, so no iteration-limit stop to observe; presupposes the autonomous-loop primitive opencode lacks. Stock opencode is turn-based (a turn ends at session.idle awaiting the next user message), with no /goal, /loop, --auto, or max-iterations cap; self-prompting is plugin-only (oh-my-opencode Ralph Loop / Ultrawork) and the upstream built-in /loop request (anomalyco/opencode#23578) is still open. With no native loop there is no iteration cap to hit. agent_supports=unknown (see assessment.json).

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: no
daemon_capability: n/a; driver_capability: n/a

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: no
daemon_capability: n/a; driver_capability: n/a

## Reasoning

This scenario exercises **iteration-cap enforcement** (per scenario-meanings: the agent CLI has a configurable max-step limit and emits a clean turn-end signal when it hits the cap; maps to `autonomous_mode` in `capabilities.json`). It is the bounded-termination variant of `autonomous-loop` — same precondition (the agent is running in a self-prompting autonomous loop), and it only becomes meaningful if that loop exists. The canonical recipe is Claude-Code-specific: it drives `/goal <unsatisfiable-condition>`, lets the loop run, then sends `/goal clear` as a stand-in for the `max_iterations` cap.

pi 0.70.0 has **no autonomous / self-prompting loop at all**, so there is no max-step cap to hit. Three independent confirmations:

1. **Binary/source keyword scan (Step 2.5).** `pi` resolves to a Node package whose real source is the bundled `dist/` JS at `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent@0.70.0`. A recursive scan of that dist for `autonomous|maxIteration|max_iteration|maxSteps|maxTurns|iterationLimit|iteration.?cap|--goal|self.?prompt|max.?loop|stepLimit` returned **zero matches**. (The `strings $(which pi)` scan on the launcher shim is empty by construction — pi is a Node script, not a compiled binary — so the dist JS scan is the authoritative equivalent and it is also empty.)

2. **Slash-command enumeration.** The full set of in-REPL slash commands extracted from the dist is `/changelog /clone /colors /compact /copy /debug /export /fork /hotkeys /import /login /logout /model /modes /name /new /quit /reload /resume /session /settings /share /theme /tree /versions` (plus easter-egg names). There is **no `/goal`-style command** and nothing that arms a self-re-prompting loop. This is the same guard that flipped Claude Code's verdict to `yes` (the `/goal` slash command was REPL-only and missed by `--help`); pi has no such hidden command.

3. **`pi --help` flags + README.** No `--goal` / `--auto` / autonomous / iteration / step / loop / max-turn flag exists; flags are model/session/tools/thinking-oriented. The README explicitly lists features pi deliberately does NOT build — sub-agents, plan mode, background bash — framing pi as a turn-driven, human-in-the-loop terminal agent.

## irrlicht_observes

Marked **n/a** rather than no: because the agent lacks the underlying autonomous loop, there is no max-iteration cap-hit arc for the daemon to observe in the first place. The pi adapter is `FilesUnderRoot` + a JSONL `Parser` whose turn boundary is `stopReason == "stop"` → `turn_done`; a single bounded turn would resolve `working → ready` normally, but that is just a plain turn, not the cap-on-an-autonomous-loop primitive this scenario asserts. Per the Step 6 table, `agent_supports: no` makes the cell `applicable: no` regardless of the observe axis. The capability key `autonomous_mode` does not exist in pi's `capabilities.json` (which has no autonomy/loop key at all), consistent with the verdict.

## Relationship to sibling cells

This tracks the `autonomous-loop` cell (also `no`, conf 0.82) one-for-one. Distinct from `token-quota-exhausted` (a cost/budget limit, partial for pi) and `turn-aborted-by-error` (an unplanned provider error, yes for pi) — both of those are different primitives and are NOT blocked by the missing autonomous loop.
caveats: If a third-party pi extension/package adds a self-prompting loop (pi's stated extension model), that is not stock pi 0.70.0 and would require re-assessment plus an `autonomous_mode` capability key.
The `strings $(which pi)` launcher scan is empty because pi is a Node script; the authoritative scan is the recursive grep over the package's `dist/` JS, which is also empty.

sources.note: recursive grep for autonomous|maxIteration|maxSteps|maxTurns|iterationLimit|--goal|self-prompt|stepLimit across pi 0.70.0 dist JS returned zero matches (Step 2.5 keyword guard, authoritative for a Node-script agent)
full slash list /changelog /clone /compact /export /fork /model /new /quit /resume /session /tree ... — no /goal or any autonomous-loop command
no --goal / --auto / iteration / step / loop / max-turn flag; flags are model/session/tools/thinking oriented
pi 'skips features like sub agents and plan mode'; turn-driven human-in-the-loop agent; no autonomous/goal mode documented
Source: FilesUnderRoot + JSONLineParser{Parser}, Match: ExactName{pi} — transport is local JSONL turns; no autonomous-loop event kind to observe
no autonomous_mode / iteration-cap capability key present for pi
